methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $5.3B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 10 same-sector + similar-valuation-tier peers in Biotech & Health. Range: $1.5B - $6.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Biotech & Health P/S median is 5.8x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health

data.narrative:
Abridge, a Series E biotech and health company founded by Shiv Rao and Zack Lipton, carries a current post-money valuation of $5.3B following $1.2B+ in total capital raised. The valuation sits within a peer set of 10 same-sector, similar-tier companies spanning both public equities (3 tickers: RXRX, TEM, ONT.L) and late-stage privates (8 companies including Tempus AI, Ultima Genomics, and Eikon Therapeutics), with peer valuations ranging from $1.5B to $6.0B.

Two methods triangulate a defensible valuation range. Stage-progression analysis, applying a typical 1.4x Series E step-up multiplier to the $5.3B base, implies $7.4B. Peer-median analysis across the 10-company comp set yields $2.3B, reflecting the wide dispersion in the comparable universe. Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples (median 5.8x across three biotech tickers) cannot be applied due to absence of disclosed revenue data, typical for private companies at this stage.

The triangulated analysis supports a range of $2.0B to $8.5B, with a median of $4.9B. The current $5.3B valuation aligns with the upper-middle of this range, slightly above the computed median. Primary uncertainties stem from the wide peer dispersion—a 4x spread from floor to ceiling—and revenue opacity that precludes multiple-based anchoring. The stage-progression and peer-median methods diverge materially ($7.4B vs. $2.3B), underscoring execution risk and comparability limitations across this emerging frontier-tech segment.
